contradiction_text: Claude Code pull requests were accepted less often than human pull requests overall, yet among merged pull requests they showed similar no-revision acceptance and revision-cost patterns.
related_claim_texts: Claude Code pull requests had a lower acceptance rate than human pull requests in the study, at 83.77425% versus 91.00529%.

Among merged pull requests, Claude Code submissions were accepted without revision at a rate similar to human pull requests, and the study found no statistically significant difference in revision frequency or measured revision cost when revisions were needed.
disclosure: Keep visible that lower overall acceptance does not translate into heavier measured revision burdens once Claude Code PRs are merged; the study shows the tension but does not establish why the acceptance gap exists.

contradiction_text: The paper reports a real acceptance gap, but the rejection record is mostly opaque because most rejected agentic pull requests were closed without explanation.
related_claim_texts: Claude Code pull requests had a lower acceptance rate than human pull requests in the study, at 83.77425% versus 91.00529%.
disclosure: Keep visible that the outcome difference is well supported, but the causal story behind rejection is substantially underobserved because most rejected cases lacked explanatory comments or discussion.

contradiction_text: Claude Code pull requests were compared against human pull requests on acceptance, but the agentic submissions had a different task mix and were much more often multi-purpose, which weakens any simple like-for-like reading of the acceptance gap.
related_claim_texts: Claude Code pull requests had a lower acceptance rate than human pull requests in the study, at 83.77425% versus 91.00529%.
Compared with human pull requests, Claude Code pull requests in the sample were more often multi-purpose and more concentrated in refactoring, documentation, and test-related work.
disclosure: Keep visible that the acceptance comparison is real, but not a clean apples-to-apples quality judgment because the observed agentic PRs were distributed differently across work types and scope.
